docs.title: EVN Observation Planner (PlanObs)
docs.text: A comprehensive tool for planning Very Long Baseline Interferometry (VLBI) observations.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The EVN Observation Planner determines source visibility, estimates sensitivity, and calculates resolution for VLBI observations. While designed for the European VLBI Network (EVN), it supports:

  • Very Long Baseline Array (VLBA)
  • Australian Long Baseline Array (LBA)
  • eMERLIN
  • Global mm-VLBI Array
  • Custom ad-hoc VLBI arrays

Online Version

Use PlanObs directly at planobs.jive.eu — no installation required!

docs.title: Features
docs.text: Feature Description Visibility Plots See when sources are observable by each antenna Sensitivity Estimation Calculate expected RMS noise levels Resolution Calculation Estimate angular resolution for your array Scheduling Generate pySCHED-compatible .key files Multiple Interfaces GUI, CLI, and Python API

docs.title: CLI Modes
docs.text:
The planobs command is organised into five modes:

Mode Command Description Observe planobs -b 6cm -t 'M87' --network EVN Plan a VLBI observation Fringe Finders planobs fringefinders -s Ef Hh Mc -t '2025-03-15 08:00' -d 8 Find bright calibrators for fringe detection Phase Calibrators planobs phasecals -t 'M87' -b 6cm Find compact calibrators near a target Source planobs source '3C273' Look up source information Server planobs server Launch the web GUI Python API 

from vlbiplanobs import Observation

obs = Observation()
obs.band = '6cm'
obs.stations = obs.stations.filter_by_network('EVN')
obs.add_target('M87')
Full programmatic control for custom workflows.

docs.title: Quick Links
docs.text:
• :material-download: Installation – Get PlanObs running locally
  • :material-rocket-launch: Quick Start – All five modes in 5 minutes
  • :material-telescope: Observation Planning – Full observe mode reference
  • :material-satellite-antenna: Fringe Finders – Find bright calibrator sources
  • :material-target-account: Phase Calibrators – Find nearby phase calibrators
  • :material-api: API Reference – Full Python API documentation

docs.title: About
docs.text:
PlanObs is developed at JIVE as the successor to the EVN Calculator. It is open source under the GPL-3.0 license.

Author: Benito Marcote (marcote@jive.eu)

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The EVN Observation Planner (PlanObs) is a comprehensive tool for planning Very Long Baseline Interferometry (VLBI) observations. It was developed at JIVE as the successor to the EVN Calculator.

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• Source Visibility – Determine when astronomical sources are visible by different antennas
  • Sensitivity Estimation – Calculate expected RMS noise levels for planned observations
  • Resolution Calculation – Estimate the angular resolution of your VLBI array
  • Schedule Generation – Create pySCHED-compatible .key files for EVN observations
  • Multiple Interfaces – GUI, CLI, and Python API for different workflows

docs.title: Supported Networks
docs.text:
PlanObs supports all major VLBI networks:

  • European VLBI Network (EVN)
  • Very Long Baseline Array (VLBA)
  • Australian Long Baseline Array (LBA)
  • eMERLIN
  • Korean VLBI Network (KVN)
  • Global mm-VLBI Array (GMVA)
  • Custom ad-hoc arrays

docs.title: Development
docs.text:
PlanObs is developed and maintained by Benito Marcote at JIVE.

  • Repository: github.com/bmarcote/vlbi_calculator
  • Issues: GitHub Issues
  • Contact: marcote@jive.eu

docs.title: Acknowledgments
docs.text:
PlanObs uses several open-source libraries:

  • Astropy – Astronomical calculations
  • Astroplan – Observation planning
  • Plotly/Dash – Interactive visualizations
  • NumPy – Numerical computing

docs.title: Citation
docs.text:
If you use PlanObs in your research, please cite:

Marcote, B. (2024). EVN Observation Planner (PlanObs).
https://github.com/bmarcote/vlbi_calculator

docs.title: CLI Reference
docs.text: The planobs command-line interface is organised into modes (subcommands). Each mode addresses a specific task in the VLBI observation planning workflow.

docs.title: Modes at a Glance
docs.text:
Command Purpose Key arguments planobs [observe] Plan a VLBI observation -b BAND, -t TARGET, -n NETWORK planobs fringefinders Find fringe finder sources -s STATIONS, -t STARTTIME, -d DURATION planobs phasecals Find phase calibrator sources -t TARGET planobs source Look up source information <source_name>, --gst planobs server Launch the web GUI --host, --port

Legacy syntax

Running planobs -b 6cm ... (without a subcommand) is equivalent to planobs observe -b 6cm ....

docs.title: Plan an observation
docs.text: planobs -b 6cm -t 'M87' --network EVN

docs.title: Find fringe finders
docs.text: planobs fringefinders -s Ef Hh Mc Tr -t '2025-03-15 08:00' -d 8 -b 6cm

docs.title: Find phase calibrators
docs.text: planobs phasecals -t 'M87' -b 6cm

docs.title: Look up a source
docs.text: planobs source '3C273'

docs.title: Start the web server
docs.text: planobs server

docs.title: Detailed Mode References
docs.text:
Each mode has its own dedicated documentation page with full argument tables, output descriptions, and worked examples:

  • Observation Planning – planobs [observe]
  • Fringe Finders – planobs fringefinders
  • Phase Calibrators – planobs phasecals
  • Source Lookup – planobs source
  • Web Server – planobs server

docs.title: List available resources
docs.text:
These flags work in the default (observe) mode and print reference data:

planobs --list-networks   # all known VLBI networks
planobs --list-antennas   # all antennas with bands and locations
planobs --list-bands      # all observing bands and supporting networks

docs.title: Custom catalogs
docs.text:
Both the observe and fringefinders modes accept custom catalogs:

planobs -b 6cm --source-catalog my_sources.toml --station-catalog my_stations.inp \
  -t MyTarget --network EVN

docs.title: JSON output
docs.text:
The fringefinders and phasecals modes support --json for machine-readable output:

planobs fringefinders -s Ef Hh Mc Tr -t '2025-03-15 08:00' -d 8 -b 6cm --json
planobs phasecals -t 'M87' -b 6cm --json

docs.title: Generate a schedule file
docs.text:
planobs -b 6cm -t 'M87' --network EVN \
  --starttime '2025-03-15 08:00' --duration 8 --sched eg123a

See Scheduling for details on the .key file format.

docs.title: Fringe Finder Sources
docs.text: The fringefinders mode searches the RFC catalog for bright calibrator sources suitable for fringe detection during a VLBI observation. These sources are used to find and correct instrumental delays between telescopes.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: planobs fringefinders -s STATIONS -t STARTTIME -d DURATION [OPTIONS]

docs.title: Quick Example
docs.text: planobs fringefinders -s Ef Hh Mc Tr -t '2025-03-15 08:00' -d 8 -b 6cm

docs.title: Required Options
docs.text: Argument Description -s, --stations List of antenna codenames or full names (e.g. Ef Hh Mc Tr). -t, --starttime Start of the observation in 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM' format (UTC). -d, --duration Duration of the observation in hours.

docs.title: Optional Options
docs.text: Argument Default Description -b, --band all bands Observing band (e.g. 6cm, 18cm). When provided, shows flux at that band only. --min-flux 0.5 Minimum unresolved flux threshold in Jy. --min-elevation 20 Minimum elevation in degrees. -l, --max-lines 20 Maximum number of sources to return. --require-all off Require source to be visible by all stations, not just any. --station-catalog built-in Path to a custom station catalog file. --json off Output results in JSON format instead of a table.

docs.title: Output
docs.text:
The tool prints a table with the following columns:

  • Name – source name from the RFC catalog.
  • IVS Name – International VLBI Service name.
  • Min elev. (deg) – minimum elevation across all specified stations.
  • Total flux (Jy) – total flux density at the observing band.
  • Unresolved (Jy) – unresolved flux density (most relevant for fringe finding).
  • Bands – bands where the source has been observed.
  • url – link to the AstroGeo database page.

When --json is used, the same data is printed as a JSON array.

docs.title: EVN fringe finders at 6 cm
docs.text:
planobs fringefinders -s Ef Hh Mc Tr Wb On -t '2025-06-15 20:00' -d 12 -b 6cm

Finds candidates for a 12-hour EVN observation at 6 cm.

docs.title: High elevation and flux requirements
docs.text:
planobs fringefinders -s Ef Hh Mc Tr -t '2025-06-15 20:00' -d 8 -b 6cm \
    --min-elevation 30 --min-flux 0.8

Returns only sources above 30° elevation with ≥ 0.8 Jy unresolved flux.

docs.title: Strict: visible by all stations, top 5 only
docs.text:
planobs fringefinders -s Ef Hh Mc Tr Ys -t '2025-06-15 20:00' -d 8 -b 6cm \
    --require-all --min-flux 1.0 -l 5

docs.title: JSON output for scripting
docs.text: planobs fringefinders -s Ef Hh Mc Tr -t '2025-03-15 08:00' -d 8 -b 6cm --json

docs.title: Tips for Good Fringe Finders
docs.text:
1. Flux – look for sources with high unresolved flux (> 0.5 Jy is typical).
  2. Elevation – higher elevation means less atmospheric absorption.
  3. Visibility – the source should be above the horizon for all stations during the observation.
  4. Sky distribution – sources spread across the sky improve delay calibration.
  5. Compactness – point-like sources (high unresolved/total ratio) are ideal.

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
No sources found – lower --min-flux, reduce --min-elevation, or remove --require-all.

Too many sources – increase --min-flux, add --require-all, or use -l to limit output.

Station not found – check the codename with planobs --list-antennas.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Python 3.11+ (required)
  • pip or conda package manager

docs.title: Install from PyPI
docs.text:
The recommended way to install PlanObs:

pip install vlbiplanobs

docs.title: Install from Source
docs.text:
For development or the latest features:

git clone https://github.com/bmarcote/vlbi_calculator.git
cd vlbi_calculator
pip install -e .

docs.title: Verify Installation
docs.text:
After installation, verify the binaries are available:

planobs --help
planobs-server --help

docs.title: Available Commands
docs.text:
After installation, the planobs command provides several modes:

Command Description planobs Observation planning (default mode) planobs fringefinders Find fringe finder sources planobs phasecals Find phase calibrator sources planobs source Look up source information planobs server Launch the web-based GUI

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text:
PlanObs automatically installs these dependencies:

  • numpy – Numerical computing
  • astropy – Astronomical calculations
  • astroplan – Observation planning
  • plotly/dash – Interactive visualizations
  • rich – Terminal formatting

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
Python Version

PlanObs requires Python 3.11 or higher. Check your version with python --version.

Virtual Environment

We recommend using a virtual environment: 

python -m venv planobs-env
source planobs-env/bin/activate  # Linux/macOS
pip install vlbiplanobs

docs.title: License
docs.text: PlanObs is released under the GNU General Public License v3.0 or later (GPL-3.0-or-later).

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
You are free to:

  • Use – Use the software for any purpose
  • Study – Study how the software works and modify it
  • Share – Copy and distribute the software
  • Improve – Improve the software and release your improvements

Under the following conditions:

  • Disclose source – Source code must be made available when distributing
  • License and copyright notice – Include the license and copyright notice
  • Same license – Modifications must be released under the same license
  • State changes – Document changes made to the code

docs.title: Full License Text
docs.text:
The full license text is available at:

  • GNU GPL v3.0
  • LICENSE file in repository

docs.title: Third-Party Licenses
docs.text:
PlanObs uses the following third-party libraries:

Library License NumPy BSD-3-Clause Astropy BSD-3-Clause Astroplan BSD-3-Clause Plotly MIT Dash MIT Rich MIT

docs.title: Observation Planning
docs.text: The observe mode is the core of PlanObs. It plans a VLBI observation by computing source visibility, estimating sensitivity, and optionally generating a pySCHED-compatible schedule file.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
planobs observe -b BAND [OPTIONS]

Legacy syntax

You can omit the observe subcommand. Running planobs -b 6cm ... is equivalent to planobs observe -b 6cm ....

docs.title: Required Arguments
docs.text:
Argument Description -b, --band Observing band (e.g. 6cm, 18cm, 1.3cm). Use --list-bands to see all options.

You must also specify at least one of --network or --stations.

docs.title: Station Selection
docs.text: Argument Description -n, --network One or more VLBI network names (e.g. EVN, eMERLIN, VLBA, LBA). Takes the default stations of each network. -s, --stations Individual station codenames or full names (e.g. Ef, Wb, Jb). Can be combined with --network. --station-catalog Path to a custom station catalog file. Overrides the built-in catalog.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
Use a predefined network:

planobs -b 6cm --network EVN

Combine a network with extra stations:

planobs -b 6cm --network EVN --stations Ar Ys

Use only specific stations:

planobs -b 6cm --stations Ef Hh Mc Tr Wb On

docs.title: Source Selection
docs.text: Argument Description -t, --targets One or more source names or coordinates. Names are resolved via SIMBAD/NED/VizieR. Coordinates use hh:mm:ss dd:mm:ss or XXhXXmXXs XXdXXmXXs format. -sc, --source-catalog Path to a TOML source catalog file. When combined with --targets, only the named blocks from the file are used.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
Observe a named source:

planobs -b 6cm -t 'M87' --network EVN

Observe a source by coordinates:

planobs -b 6cm -t '12h30m49.4s +12d23m28s' --network EVN

Use a source catalog:

planobs -b 6cm --source-catalog my_sources.toml --network EVN

Select specific blocks from the catalog:

planobs -b 6cm --source-catalog my_sources.toml -t block1 block2 --network EVN

docs.title: Time and Duration
docs.text:
Argument Description -t1, --starttime Start of the observation in 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM' format (UTC). -d, --duration Total duration of the observation in hours.

When no time is specified, PlanObs searches for the optimal GST range and reports when the source is observable.

When both start time and duration are given, PlanObs evaluates visibility and sensitivity for that specific epoch.

Warning

If you provide --starttime, you must also provide --duration.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
No specific time (find optimal GST range):

planobs -b 6cm -t 'M87' --network EVN

Fixed epoch observation:

planobs -b 6cm -t 'M87' --network EVN --starttime '2025-06-15 08:00' --duration 12

docs.title: Setup Options
docs.text: Argument Description --data-rate Maximum data rate in Mb/s. If omitted, PlanObs infers it from the network.

docs.title: Scheduling
docs.text: Argument Description --sched Generate a pySCHED .key schedule file. The value is used as the experiment code and filename. --fringefinders Fringe finder source(s) or a number of automatic selections (default: 2). --polcal Include polarization calibration scans. --pulsar Include pulsar source scans. Accepts a source name or a number referencing the source catalog.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Generate a schedule file with automatic fringe finder selection:

planobs -b 6cm -t 'M87' --network EVN \
  --starttime '2025-03-15 08:00' --duration 8 \
  --sched eg123a

This creates eg123a.key. See Scheduling for details on the .key file format.

docs.title: Output Options
docs.text: Argument Description --gui Open graphical plots in the browser. --no-tui Suppress the terminal (TUI) output. --debug Show debug messages and execution time.

docs.title: Information Commands
docs.text:
These print reference data and exit immediately:

Argument Description --list-antennas List all antennas in the catalog with their bands and locations. --list-networks List all known VLBI networks and their default stations. --list-bands List all observing bands and which networks support them. 
planobs --list-networks
planobs --list-antennas
planobs --list-bands

docs.title: Output
docs.text:
When run with a target source, PlanObs prints:

  1. Observation summary – band, duration, stations, setup (data rate, bandwidth, subbands).
  2. Source list – all sources in every scan block, with coordinates.
  3. Visibility chart – a colour-coded plotext scatter plot showing each station's elevation over time (red < 10°, yellow 10–20°, green 20–40°, cyan 40–60°, blue > 60°).
  4. Optimal observing window – UTC or GST range when all antennas can observe simultaneously, and when ≥ 3 antennas can observe simultaneously.
  5. Thermal noise estimate – expected RMS noise for the target, with on-source time.
  6. Sun constraint warning – if the Sun is too close to the source during the observation.

When run without a target (only band + stations + time), PlanObs reports the expected thermal noise for a hypothetical source at ±45° elevation.

docs.title: Quick visibility check (no fixed time)
docs.text: planobs -b 6cm -t 'Cygnus A' --network EVN eMERLIN

docs.title: Detailed observation at a fixed epoch
docs.text:
planobs -b 6cm \
  -t 'Cygnus A' \
  --network EVN eMERLIN \
  --stations Ar Ys \
  --starttime '2025-06-15 08:00' \
  --duration 12 \
  --data-rate 2048

docs.title: Generate a full schedule
docs.text:
planobs -b 6cm \
  -t 'M87' \
  --network EVN \
  --starttime '2025-03-15 08:00' \
  --duration 8 \
  --sched eg123a \
  --fringefinders 3 \
  --polcal

docs.title: Custom catalogs
docs.text:
planobs -b 6cm \
  --source-catalog my_sources.toml \
  --station-catalog my_stations.inp \
  -t MyTarget \
  --network EVN

docs.title: Terminal-only output (no GUI)
docs.text: planobs -b 6cm -t 'M87' --network EVN --no-tui

docs.title: Web Server (GUI)
docs.text: The server mode launches the PlanObs web interface — the same application hosted at planobs.jive.eu. It runs a local Dash/Plotly server you can access in your browser.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: planobs server [OPTIONS]

docs.title: Options
docs.text: Argument Default Description --host 127.0.0.1 Network interface to bind to. Use 0.0.0.0 to allow access from other machines. --port 8050 TCP port number. --debug off Enable Dash debug mode (auto-reload on code changes, detailed error pages).

docs.title: Start with defaults
docs.text:
planobs server

Then open http://localhost:8050 in your browser.

docs.title: Expose on the local network
docs.text:
planobs server --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8080

Other machines on the same network can access the GUI at http://<your-ip>:8080.

docs.title: Development mode
docs.text:
planobs server --debug

The server auto-reloads when source files change and shows detailed tracebacks on errors.

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
The web GUI provides interactive versions of the same capabilities available through the CLI:

  • Source visibility plots – interactive elevation plots for each station.
  • Sensitivity calculator – expected thermal noise based on the array and setup.
  • Resolution estimator – angular resolution for the selected stations and band.
  • Station map – geographic map of the participating antennas.

docs.title: Antenna Selection
docs.text:
The antenna panel lets you select stations individually or by network.

  • Single-configuration antennas appear as standard toggle chips. Click to include/exclude from the observation.
  • Multi-configuration antennas (VLA, MeerKAT, SKAO) appear as a split-button chip:
  • The chip label shows the group name (e.g. VLA).
  • The ▼ button on the right opens a dropdown to switch between configurations (e.g. single dish vs. phased array). Hovering over each option shows the full antenna information card.
  • Selecting any configuration from the dropdown automatically includes that antenna in the observation.
  • The currently active configuration is highlighted in the dropdown menu.
  • Only one configuration per group can be active at a time.
  • Chips dim automatically when the active configuration does not support the selected observing band.
  • Network switches select/deselect all antennas in a predefined network at once, including the appropriate configuration for grouped antennas.

docs.title: When to Use This Mode
docs.text:
• Interactive exploration – experiment with different arrays, bands, and times without re-running CLI commands.
  • Presentations and teaching – the graphical output is suitable for talks and tutorials.
  • Remote access – bind to 0.0.0.0 to share the planner with colleagues on your network.

docs.title: Source Lookup
docs.text: The source mode retrieves detailed information about a single source. It queries the RFC (Radio Fundamental Catalog) and, if the source is not found there, falls back to SIMBAD/NED/VizieR.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
planobs source [options] <source_name>

The source_name argument is required and positional. It can be:

  • A J2000 name from the RFC catalog (e.g. J1229+0203)
  • An IVS name (e.g. 3C273)
  • Any source resolvable by SIMBAD/NED/VizieR (e.g. M87)

docs.title: Options
docs.text: Flag Description --no-networks Skip the network observability table. --gst Append a GST column to the observability table showing the Greenwich Sidereal Time ranges (HH:MM-HH:MM) during which more than 3 antennas in the network can observe the source.

docs.title: Source found in RFC catalog
docs.text:
When the source is in the RFC catalog, PlanObs prints:

  • Name and aliases – J2000 name and IVS name.
  • Coordinates – Right ascension and declination in HMS/DMS format.
  • Number of observations – how many times the source has been observed in geodetic/astrometric VLBI.
  • Flux table – total and unresolved flux density at each band where data is available.
  • AstroGeo link – direct URL to the source page on the AstroGeo database.

The flux table includes these bands:

Band Wavelength S 18/21 cm C 13/6/5 cm X 3.6 cm U 2 cm K 1.3/0.7 cm

docs.title: Source not in RFC catalog
docs.text:
If the source is not found in the RFC catalog but can be resolved by SIMBAD/NED/VizieR, PlanObs prints:

  • Name – as returned by the resolver.
  • Coordinates – right ascension and declination.
  • A note that the source is not in the RFC calibrators catalog.

If the source cannot be found at all, PlanObs prints an error and exits with code 1.

docs.title: Look up a well-known calibrator
docs.text: planobs source '3C273'

docs.title: Look up by J2000 name
docs.text: planobs source 'J1229+0203'

docs.title: Look up a non-RFC source
docs.text: planobs source 'M87'

docs.title: Show GST observing windows
docs.text:
planobs source '3C273' --gst

The table gains a GST column showing the sidereal time range(s) when each network has more than 3 antennas able to observe the source (e.g. 01:15-19:30).

docs.title: When to Use This Mode
docs.text:
• Before planning an observation – check whether a potential calibrator has sufficient flux.
  • Evaluating calibrators – compare total vs. unresolved flux to judge compactness.
  • Quick coordinate lookup – get precise J2000 coordinates for any source.

docs.title: Phase Calibrator Sources
docs.text: The phasecals mode searches the RFC catalog for compact calibrator sources near your target, suitable for phase-referenced VLBI observations. Phase calibrators correct atmospheric and instrumental phase variations.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: planobs phasecals -t TARGET [OPTIONS]

docs.title: Quick Example
docs.text: planobs phasecals -t 'M87' -b 6cm

docs.title: Required Options
docs.text: Argument Description -t, --target Target source name. Accepts J2000 names, IVS names from the RFC catalog, or any name resolvable by SIMBAD/NED/VizieR.

docs.title: Optional Options
docs.text: Argument Default Description -b, --band all bands Observing band (e.g. 6cm, 1.3cm). When omitted, shows average flux across all bands. --max-separation 5.0 Maximum angular separation from the target in degrees. --min-flux 0.1 Minimum unresolved flux threshold in Jy. -n, --n-sources all Maximum number of sources to return (sorted by separation). --catalog-file built-in Path to a custom RFC catalog file. --json off Output results in JSON format instead of a table.

docs.title: Output
docs.text:
The tool prints a table with the following columns:

  • Name – source name from the RFC catalog.
  • IVS Name – International VLBI Service name.
  • Separation (deg) – angular distance from the target.
  • Total Flux (Jy) – total flux density at the observing band.
  • Unresolved (Jy) – unresolved flux density (most relevant for phase calibration).
  • Bands – bands where the source has been observed.
  • url – link to the AstroGeo database page.

When --json is used, the same data is printed as a JSON array.

docs.title: Basic search at 6 cm
docs.text:
planobs phasecals -t 'M87' -b 6cm

Finds all calibrator candidates within 5° of M87 at 6 cm.

docs.title: Tight separation constraint
docs.text:
planobs phasecals -t 'M87' -b 6cm --max-separation 2 --min-flux 0.2

Calibrators within 2° with ≥ 0.2 Jy unresolved flux.

docs.title: All-band search, top 10
docs.text:
planobs phasecals -t '3C273' --max-separation 10 -n 10

Searches all bands for calibrators within 10° of 3C273, returns the 10 closest.

docs.title: High-frequency observation
docs.text:
planobs phasecals -t 'NGC 4258' -b 1.3cm --max-separation 3 --min-flux 0.5

Stricter flux requirements for a 1.3 cm observation.

docs.title: JSON output for scripting
docs.text: planobs phasecals -t 'M87' -b 6cm --json

docs.title: Separation distance
docs.text: Separation Quality < 1° Excellent — minimal phase decorrelation 1–3° Good — commonly used range 3–5° Acceptable at lower frequencies > 5° Risk of significant decorrelation, especially at high frequencies

docs.title: Flux requirements by frequency
docs.text: Frequency range Recommended minimum flux ≥ 6 cm 0.1–0.2 Jy 3–6 cm 0.2–0.5 Jy ≤ 2 cm 0.5–1.0+ Jy

docs.title: Source compactness
docs.text: A high unresolved/total flux ratio indicates a compact source. Extended structure introduces phase errors — always check the AstroGeo link for source maps.

docs.title: Tips
docs.text:
1. Proximity – choose the closest suitable calibrator.
  2. Flux – ensure sufficient flux for your band and integration time.
  3. Compactness – prefer high unresolved/total flux ratios.
  4. Elevation – consider the calibrator elevation during your observation.
  5. Backup – identify at least one backup calibrator.

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
No sources found – increase --max-separation, lower --min-flux, or verify the target name.

Target not found – check spelling, try coordinates, or verify with planobs source <name>.

Too many weak sources – increase --min-flux, reduce --max-separation, or use -n to limit output.

docs.title: Integration with Observation Planning
docs.text:
Once you have identified calibrators, add them to your source catalog file and include them in the observation plan:

planobs -b 6cm --source-catalog my_sources.toml --network EVN \
  --starttime '2025-06-15 08:00' --duration 8

PlanObs automatically computes phase-referencing cycle times based on the observing band.

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text: Get started with PlanObs in under 5 minutes. The planobs command provides five modes, each designed for a specific task in the VLBI observation planning workflow.

docs.title: Modes Overview
docs.text:
Mode Command Purpose Observe planobs -b BAND ... or planobs observe ... Plan a VLBI observation: visibility, sensitivity, scheduling Fringe Finders planobs fringefinders ... Find bright calibrator sources for fringe detection Phase Calibrators planobs phasecals ... Find compact calibrators near your target Source planobs source <name> Look up detailed information about a source Server planobs server Launch the web-based GUI

Legacy syntax

Running planobs without a subcommand (e.g. planobs -b 6cm ...) is equivalent to planobs observe -b 6cm .... Both forms work.

docs.title: 1. Observe – Plan a VLBI Observation
docs.text:
Check when a source is visible with the EVN and estimate the expected sensitivity:

planobs -b 6cm -t 'M87' --network EVN

This prints source visibility per antenna, expected thermal noise, and optimal observing windows.

:material-arrow-right: Full observe reference

docs.title: 2. Fringe Finders – Find Calibrator Sources
docs.text:
Find bright, compact sources visible by your stations during the observation, suitable for fringe detection:

planobs fringefinders -s Ef Hh Mc Tr -t '2025-03-15 08:00' -d 8 -b 6cm

Returns a ranked table of candidates with flux densities, elevations, and AstroGeo links.

:material-arrow-right: Full fringefinders reference

docs.title: 3. Phase Calibrators – Find Nearby Calibrators
docs.text:
Search for phase calibrator candidates close to your target:

planobs phasecals -t 'M87' -b 6cm

Returns calibrators within the default 5° separation, sorted by distance.

:material-arrow-right: Full phasecals reference

docs.title: 4. Source – Look Up Source Information
docs.text:
Retrieve RFC catalog data (flux densities, coordinates, AstroGeo link) for any known source:

planobs source '3C273'

If the source is not in the RFC catalog, PlanObs resolves it via SIMBAD/NED/VizieR.

Add --gst to see the GST time ranges when each network has enough antennas to observe the source:

planobs source '3C273' --gst

:material-arrow-right: Full source reference

docs.title: 5. Server – Launch the Web GUI
docs.text:
Start the interactive web interface (identical to planobs.jive.eu):

planobs server

Then open your browser to http://localhost:8050.

:material-arrow-right: Full server reference

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Observation Planning – Complete guide to the observe mode
  • Scheduling – Generate pySCHED .key files
  • CLI Overview – All modes and common patterns at a glance
  • API Reference – Python API documentation

docs.title: New Features
docs.text:
• Grouped antenna chips in GUI – Antennas with multiple configurations (e.g. VLA, MeerKAT, SKAO) are now collapsed into a single split-button chip. The chip label shows the group name; a ▼ dropdown lets you switch between configurations. Picking a configuration automatically selects the antenna for the observation. The active configuration is highlighted in blue in the dropdown.
  • Azimuth-dependent local horizon – Station-specific terrain and structure blockage is now modelled for 25 stations (Effelsberg, VLBA, Urumqi/Nanshan, Robledo, and others) using pySCHED's horizon data. Elevations are correctly excluded when below the local horizon at a given azimuth.
  • Terminal elevation plot – The CLI per-source visibility output now uses a plotext scatter plot with colour-coded elevation bands (red < 10°, yellow 10–20°, green 20–40°, cyan 40–60°, blue > 60°) instead of hand-drawn character rows.
  • Phase calibrator and check-source selection – planobs observe now accepts --phasecal and --check-source options for automatic or named calibrator selection.
  • Fringe finder improvements – planobs fringefinders accepts -n/--network to filter by network; fringe finder scheduling uses round-robin distribution across multiple sources.
  • Polarisation calibration – --polcal schedules 3C84/OQ208/DA193 blocks spread at 10 %/50 %/90 % of available time.
  • Name/coordinate source parsing – All source arguments accept a name/RA Dec syntax (e.g. 'MySrc/12:30:49 +12:23:28') where user-supplied coordinates override any catalog lookup.

docs.title: Improvements
docs.text:
• Renamed Urumqi station display name to Nanshan (its current operational name).
  • Added group field to station catalog entries for VLA (Y1, Y27), MeerKAT (Me1, Me), and SKAO (Sk1, Sk2, Sk4).
  • Observability summary wording clarified: "All antennas can observe the block simultaneously" and "Optimal visibility window".
  • GUI chip alignment: grouped chips now match dmc.Chip height, font size, and weight exactly.
  • ortools >= 9.0 is now a hard dependency (was commented out).

docs.title: Bug Fixes
docs.text:
• enable_antennas_with_band callback crashed on initial page load when band_index was None.
  • Network switches could add both grouped codenames (e.g. Y1 and Y27) simultaneously to the selection, violating the single-active-config invariant.
  • Grouped chip dropdown highlight was reset on observation re-render; fixed by using CSS class patching instead of Mantine prop updates.

docs.title: API Changes
docs.text:
• Station gained an optional group: Optional[str] parameter and property for GUI grouping.
  • Station gained an optional horizon: Optional[tuple[np.ndarray, np.ndarray]] parameter and horizon_min_elevation(az) method.
  • Source.parse_source_spec(spec) static method added for name/coord parsing.
  • calibrators.select_phase_calibrator() and calibrators.select_check_source() added.

docs.title: New Features
docs.text:
• Observation Scheduler – New ObservationScheduler class for automated scan scheduling
  • Schedule File Generation – Generate pySCHED-compatible .key files via --sched CLI option
  • Fringe Finder Scheduling – Automatic placement of fringe finders (2 at start, 1 at end, ~2h intervals)

docs.title: Improvements
docs.text:
• Optimized scheduler code with reduced memory footprint
  • NumPy-style docstrings across all modules
  • Complete MkDocs documentation

docs.title: API Changes
docs.text:
• Added Observation.schedule_file() method
  • Added ObservationScheduler class in vlbiplanobs.scheduler
  • Added ScheduledScanBlock dataclass

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• Modern Dash-based GUI
  • Real-time visibility calculations
  • Interactive elevation plots
  • Multiple network support

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• Initial Python 3.11+ support
  • CLI interface (planobs command)
  • Source resolution via SIMBAD/NED/VizieR

docs.title: From 4.5.x to 4.6.x
docs.text: No breaking changes. New scheduling features are additive.

docs.title: From EVN Calculator
docs.text:
PlanObs is a complete rewrite. Key differences:

  1. Local Installation – Can run offline
  2. Python API – Full programmatic access
  3. Multiple Outputs – GUI, CLI, and schedule files
  4. Extended Networks – Support for non-EVN arrays

docs.title: Scheduling
docs.text: PlanObs includes a scheduler that generates pySCHED-compatible .key files for EVN observations.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The scheduler arranges scan blocks across your observation following VLBI conventions:

  • Fringe finders: 2× at start, 1× at end, every ~2h for long observations
  • Science blocks: Optimized for antenna participation and elevation

docs.title: Via CLI
docs.text:
planobs -b 6cm -t 'M87' --network EVN \
  --starttime '2025-03-15 08:00' \
  --duration 8 \
  --sched my_experiment

This creates my_experiment.key.

docs.title: Via Python
docs.text:
from vlbiplanobs import Observation

obs = Observation()
# ... configure observation ...

key_content = obs.schedule_file(
    experiment_code='EG123A',
    pi_name='Your Name',
    pi_email='you@example.com',
    pi_institute='Your Institute',
    setup_file='evn6cm-2Gbps-32MHz.set'
)

with open('eg123a.key', 'w') as f:
    f.write(key_content)

docs.title: Schedule File Format
docs.text:
The generated .key file follows the pySCHED format:

! pySCHED key file generated by VLBI Planning Observation Calculator

! ==================== Cover Information ==============================
expcode  = 'EG123A'
piname   = 'Your Name'
email    = 'you@example.com'

! ==================== Correlator Information =========================
obstype  = 'VLBI'
correl   = 'JIVE'
cornant  = 8

! ==================== Source Catalog =================================
srccat /
  source='M87' ra=12:30:49.4234 dec=+12:23:28.044 equinox='J2000' /
endcat /

! ==================== The Scans ======================================
stations = EF, WB, JB, ON, MC, NT, TR, YS

source='3C279' gap=0:00 dur=5:00 /
source='3C279' gap=0:00 dur=5:00 /
source='M87' gap=0:00 dur=14:30 /
...
source='3C279' gap=0:00 dur=5:00 /

docs.title: Using with pySCHED
docs.text:
After generating the .key file:

sched.py -k my_experiment.key

This produces the VEX file and other outputs needed for correlation.

docs.title: Fringe Finder Rules
docs.text:
Duration Fringe Finders < 3 hours 2 at start, 1 at end ≥ 3 hours 2 at start, 1 at end, + every ~2h

Each fringe finder scan is 5 minutes.

docs.title: Science Block Optimization
docs.text:
The scheduler optimizes science blocks for:

  1. Maximum antennas – Prioritizes times when most stations can observe
  2. Highest elevation – Secondary optimization for better sensitivity

docs.title: Python API
docs.text:
from vlbiplanobs.scheduler import ObservationScheduler

scheduler = ObservationScheduler(
    observation,
    min_antennas=3,           # Require at least 3 antennas
    require_all_antennas=True # All stations must observe
)

schedule = scheduler.schedule()
scheduler.print_schedule()

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• EVN Schedule Preparation
  • NRAO SCHED Manual

docs.title: Tutorial
docs.text:
A step-by-step walkthrough covering the most common PlanObs workflows.

Tip

For a quick overview of every mode with one-liner examples, see the Quick Start.

docs.title: Check source visibility (no fixed time)
docs.text:
Find when a source is observable by the EVN and eMERLIN:

planobs -b 6cm -t 'Altair' --network EVN eMERLIN

PlanObs scans the full GST range and reports optimal observing windows.

docs.title: Plan at a specific epoch
docs.text:
planobs -b 6cm -t 'Altair' --stations Ef Hh Ir Mc Tr T6 O8 Wb Cm \
  --starttime '2025-06-15 20:00' --duration 12

This evaluates visibility and sensitivity for the given 12-hour window.

docs.title: Suppress the GUI
docs.text:
Add --no-tui to skip the graphical elevation plots and keep only the terminal output:

planobs -b 6cm -t 'Altair' --network EVN --no-tui

:material-arrow-right: Full reference: Observation Planning

docs.title: 2. Find Fringe Finder Sources
docs.text:
Fringe finders are bright calibrators used to detect and correct instrumental delays. Use the fringefinders mode:

planobs fringefinders -s Ef Hh Mc Tr -t '2025-03-15 08:00' -d 8 -b 6cm

This searches the RFC catalog and returns a ranked table of candidates with flux densities, elevations, and AstroGeo links.

:material-arrow-right: Full reference: Fringe Finders

docs.title: 3. Find Phase Calibrators
docs.text:
Phase calibrators are compact sources near the target used for phase referencing:

planobs phasecals -t 'M87' -b 6cm --max-separation 5 --min-flux 0.1

Returns calibrators within 5° of M87 with ≥ 0.1 Jy unresolved flux.

:material-arrow-right: Full reference: Phase Calibrators

docs.title: 4. Look Up Source Information
docs.text:
Query the RFC catalog (or SIMBAD/NED/VizieR) for any source:

planobs source '3C273'

Prints coordinates, flux table across bands, number of observations, and the AstroGeo link.

:material-arrow-right: Full reference: Source Lookup

docs.title: 5. Generate a Schedule File
docs.text:
Create a pySCHED-compatible .key file by adding --sched:

planobs -b 6cm -t 'M87' --network EVN \
  --starttime '2025-03-15 08:00' --duration 8 \
  --sched eg123a

This produces eg123a.key. You can control fringe finder selection and add calibration scans:

planobs -b 6cm -t 'M87' --network EVN \
  --starttime '2025-03-15 08:00' --duration 8 \
  --sched eg123a --fringefinders 3 --polcal

:material-arrow-right: Full reference: Scheduling

docs.title: 6. Customising the Station Catalog
docs.text:
Station data is stored in data/stations_catalog.inp using Python configparser format. To add a station, create a new section:

[Station Name]
station = Station Name
code = XX
network = EVN
possible_networks = EVN, GMVA
country = Netherlands
diameter = 25 m
position = X, Y, Z
min_elevation = 10
real_time = yes
SEFD_6 = 420
SEFD_18 = 700
Field Description code Unique codename (e.g. Ef, Wb). network Primary network, or Other. possible_networks Comma-separated list of networks the station can join. position Geocentric X, Y, Z coordinates. min_elevation Minimum observable elevation in degrees (default 10). real_time yes if the station supports e-VLBI. SEFD_YY System Equivalent Flux Density at wavelength YY cm (in Jy). One entry per band.

Use --station-catalog to load your custom file:

planobs -b 6cm --station-catalog my_stations.inp --network EVN -t 'M87'

We welcome contributions of additional stations — please open an issue or pull request on GitHub.

docs.title: API Reference
docs.text: Complete Python API documentation for vlbiplanobs.

docs.title: Observation
docs.text:
The main class for planning VLBI observations.

Full Observation Documentation →

docs.title: Stations
docs.text:
Classes for managing VLBI stations and networks.

Full Stations Documentation →

docs.title: Sources
docs.text:
Classes for astronomical sources and scan blocks.

Full Sources Documentation →

docs.title: Scheduler
docs.text:
Observation scheduling for pySCHED output.

Full Scheduler Documentation →

docs.title: Quick Example
docs.text:
from vlbiplanobs import Observation
from astropy.time import Time
from astropy import units as u

# Create and configure observation
obs = Observation()
obs.band = '6cm'
obs.times = Time('2025-03-15 08:00') + (range(0, 481, 5) * u.min)
obs.stations = obs.stations.filter_by_network('EVN')
obs.add_target('M87')

# Get results
print(f"Sensitivity: {obs.sensitivity()}")
print(f"Resolution: {obs.angular_resolution()}")

# Generate schedule file
key_content = obs.schedule_file(experiment_code='EG123A')

docs.title: Module Index
docs.text: Module Description vlbiplanobs.observation Main Observation class vlbiplanobs.stations Station and Stations classes vlbiplanobs.sources Source, Scan, ScanBlock classes vlbiplanobs.scheduler ObservationScheduler for pySCHED output vlbiplanobs.freqsetups Frequency setup definitions vlbiplanobs.rfc Radio Fundamental Catalog interface

docs.title: Observation
docs.text: The Observation class is the main entry point for planning VLBI observations.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
from vlbiplanobs import Observation

obs = Observation()
obs.band = '6cm'
obs.add_target('M87')

docs.title: Observing Setup
docs.text:
• band - Observing wavelength (e.g., '6cm', '18cm')
  • times - Array of observation times
  • stations - Stations participating in the observation
  • datarate - Data rate in Mbit/s

docs.title: Sources
docs.text:
• scans - Dictionary of scan blocks
  • sources() - Get all sources in the observation

docs.title: Results
docs.text:
• sensitivity() - Calculate expected RMS noise
  • angular_resolution() - Calculate synthesized beam size
  • is_observable() - Check source visibility per station

docs.title: Basic Observation
docs.text:
from vlbiplanobs import Observation
from astropy.time import Time
from astropy import units as u

obs = Observation()
obs.band = '6cm'
obs.times = Time('2025-03-15 08:00') + (range(0, 481, 5) * u.min)
obs.stations = obs.stations.filter_by_network('EVN')
obs.add_target('M87')

# Check visibility
visibility = obs.is_observable()
elevations = obs.elevations()

docs.title: Generate Schedule File
docs.text:
key_content = obs.schedule_file(
    experiment_code='EG123A',
    pi_name='Your Name',
    pi_email='you@example.com'
)

with open('eg123a.key', 'w') as f:
    f.write(key_content)

docs.title: Multiple Targets
docs.text:
obs.add_target('M87')
obs.add_target('3C273')
obs.add_target('Cygnus A')

for name, block in obs.scans.items():
    print(f"{name}: {block.sources()}")

docs.title: Scheduler
docs.text: Observation scheduling for generating pySCHED-compatible files.

docs.title: ObservationScheduler
docs.text: Main class for scheduling VLBI observations.

docs.title: Scheduling Rules
docs.text:
Fringe Finders:

  • 2× 5-minute scans at observation start
  • 1× 5-minute scan at observation end
  • For observations > 3 hours: additional scans every ~2 hours

Science Blocks:

  • Scheduled in gaps between fringe finders
  • Optimized for maximum antenna participation
  • Secondary optimization for highest elevation

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
from vlbiplanobs.scheduler import ObservationScheduler

# Create scheduler
scheduler = ObservationScheduler(
    observation,
    min_antennas=3,
    require_all_antennas=False
)

# Generate schedule
schedule = scheduler.schedule()

# View results
scheduler.print_schedule()

# Get detailed block info
for block in scheduler.get_scheduled_blocks():
    print(f"{block.name}: {block.start_time.iso} - {block.end_time.iso}")
    print(f"  Antennas: {block.n_antennas}, Elevation: {block.mean_elevation:.1f}°")

docs.title: ScheduledScanBlock
docs.text: Dataclass representing a scheduled scan block with timing metadata.

docs.title: Properties
docs.text:
• name - Block identifier (e.g., 'FF_start_1', 'Target_A')
  • block - Original ScanBlock
  • start_time - Scheduled start time
  • end_time - Scheduled end time
  • duration - Block duration
  • scans - Expanded list of individual scans
  • n_antennas - Number of participating antennas
  • mean_elevation - Mean source elevation (degrees)

docs.title: Integration with Observation
docs.text:
The scheduler is automatically used when generating schedule files:

from vlbiplanobs import Observation

obs = Observation()
# ... configure observation ...

# This internally uses ObservationScheduler
key_content = obs.schedule_file(
    experiment_code='EG123A',
    pi_name='Your Name'
)

docs.title: Manual Scheduling
docs.text:
For more control over the scheduling process:

from vlbiplanobs.scheduler import ObservationScheduler

scheduler = ObservationScheduler(obs, min_antennas=4, require_all_antennas=True)
schedule_dict = scheduler.schedule()

# Access scheduled blocks
blocks = scheduler.get_scheduled_blocks()

for block in blocks:
    print(f"{block.name}:")
    print(f"  Time: {block.start_time.iso} to {block.end_time.iso}")
    print(f"  Duration: {block.duration}")
    print(f"  Antennas: {block.n_antennas}")
    print(f"  Scans: {len(block.scans)}")

docs.title: Sources
docs.text: Classes for astronomical sources and observation scans.

docs.title: Source
docs.text: Represents an astronomical source to observe.

docs.title: Source Types
docs.text:
from vlbiplanobs.sources import SourceType

SourceType.TARGET        # Science target
SourceType.PHASECAL      # Phase calibrator
SourceType.FRINGEFINDER  # Fringe finder
SourceType.AMPLITUDECAL  # Amplitude calibrator
SourceType.CHECKSOURCE   # Check source
SourceType.POLCAL        # Polarization calibrator

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
from vlbiplanobs.sources import Source, SourceType
from astropy.coordinates import SkyCoord

# Create from coordinates
src = Source(
    name='M87',
    coord=SkyCoord('12h30m49.4s', '+12d23m28s'),
    type=SourceType.TARGET
)

# Resolve by name (uses SIMBAD/NED)
src = Source.from_name('Cygnus A', type=SourceType.TARGET)

docs.title: Scan
docs.text: A single observation of a source for a specific duration.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
from vlbiplanobs.sources import Scan
from astropy import units as u

scan = Scan(source=my_source, duration=10*u.min)

docs.title: ScanBlock
docs.text: A collection of scans to be observed together.

docs.title: Key Methods
docs.text:
• fill(duration) - Expand scans to fill available time
  • sources(type) - Get sources of a specific type
  • has(type) - Check if block contains a source type

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
from vlbiplanobs.sources import ScanBlock, Scan, Source, SourceType
from astropy import units as u

# Create scans
target = Scan(Source.from_name('M87', SourceType.TARGET), duration=10*u.min)
phasecal = Scan(Source.from_name('J1230+1223', SourceType.PHASECAL), duration=2*u.min)

# Create block
block = ScanBlock([phasecal, target])

# Check contents
print(f"Has target: {block.has(SourceType.TARGET)}")
print(f"Has fringe finder: {block.has(SourceType.FRINGEFINDER)}")

# Fill time slot
expanded_scans = block.fill(60*u.min)

docs.title: Stations
docs.text: Classes for managing VLBI stations and networks.

docs.title: Station
docs.text: Represents a single radio telescope.

docs.title: Key Properties
docs.text:
• name - Full station name
  • codename - Two-letter station code (e.g., 'Ef', 'Wb')
  • networks - Networks this station belongs to
  • location - Geographic coordinates
  • bands - Available observing bands
  • group - Group identifier for multi-configuration antennas (e.g. 'VLA'). None for standalone stations. Used by the GUI to collapse configurations into a single chip.
  • horizon - Azimuth-dependent local horizon as a (az_deg, el_deg) tuple of NumPy arrays. None if no horizon data is available. Used to exclude elevations below terrain/structure blockage at a given azimuth.
  • horizon_min_elevation(az) - Returns the minimum observable elevation (degrees) at a given azimuth. Returns 0 if no horizon is defined.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
from vlbiplanobs.stations import Stations

all_stations = Stations()
effelsberg = all_stations['Ef']

print(f"Name: {effelsberg.name}")
print(f"Location: {effelsberg.location}")
print(f"Bands: {effelsberg.bands}")

docs.title: Stations
docs.text: Collection of Station objects with filtering capabilities.

docs.title: Key Methods
docs.text:
• filter_by_network(network) - Get stations in a network
  • filter_by_band(band) - Get stations that can observe a band
  • filter_antennas(codenames) - Get specific stations by code

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
from vlbiplanobs.stations import Stations

# Load all stations
stations = Stations()

# Filter by network
evn = stations.filter_by_network('EVN')
print(f"EVN stations: {evn.station_codenames}")

# Filter by band
cm6_capable = stations.filter_by_band('6cm')

# Combine networks
combined = stations.filter_by_network(['EVN', 'eMERLIN'])

docs.title: Available Networks
docs.text: Network Description EVN European VLBI Network eMERLIN Enhanced Multi Element Remotely Linked Interferometer Network VLBA Very Long Baseline Array LBA Australian Long Baseline Array KVN Korean VLBI Network GMVA Global mm-VLBI Array

docs.title: Station Codes
docs.text:
Common EVN station codes:

Code Station Ef Effelsberg (Germany) Wb Westerbork (Netherlands) Jb Jodrell Bank (UK) On Onsala (Sweden) Mc Medicina (Italy) Nt Noto (Italy) Tr Torun (Poland) Ys Yebes (Spain) Hh Hartebeesthoek (South Africa) Sh Shanghai (China)
